slides.narration: Let's unpack how async and await actually work. By the end you'll see they're just a nicer syntax for promises.

data.title: How async/await really works
data.subtitle: Promises, the event loop, and what await actually does

slides.narration: First, the one sentence definition. An async function returns a promise, and await pauses that function until the promise settles.

data.definition: Syntax that lets you write non-blocking code as if it were sequential, by pausing a function until a promise resolves.

slides.narration: Why care? Three reasons this matters in everyday code.

data.heading: Why it matters
items: Errors work with plain try / catch
Parallel work becomes explicit, not accidental

slides.narration: Here's the key insight most tutorials bury. Async and await aren't a new runtime feature — they're syntax sugar on top of promises.

data.title: Key insight
data.body: async/await is pure syntax sugar over Promises — nothing new at runtime.

slides.narration: The minimum you need. Mark a function async, and you can await any promise inside. The return value is wrapped in a promise automatically.

data.heading: The simplest async function
data.caption: Highlighted lines are what async adds.

slides.narration: So what really happens on await? The function leaves the call stack, the runtime does the pending work, and when the promise settles the function resumes.
Same story, but as an interaction. The caller invokes the async function, which kicks off a fetch, control returns to the event loop, and resumption happens when the response lands.
Before async, chaining many network calls meant nested callbacks or long promise chains. With async, it reads flat.
If you're converting legacy callback code, here's the short recipe. Four steps.

steps.title: Wrap the API
steps.detail: Expose a Promise-returning version of the callback API.

steps.title: Mark callers async
steps.detail: Add the async keyword so you can use await inside.

slides.narration: You can test your async code right in the terminal. Modern Node supports top level await in ES module files.
A typical project layout. The interesting bit lives in services, where async functions wrap your data access.
Mental model check. Synchronous code waits and blocks. Async code yields, letting other work happen while it waits.

data.leftBody: Each line blocks until it finishes. Simple but stalls the whole program.
data.rightBody: Awaited tasks yield control. Other code runs while you wait on I/O.

slides.narration: Two traps beginners hit constantly. Forgetting await, which returns a promise object instead of a value. And unhandled rejections, which can crash your process.

data.title: Common pitfalls
pitfalls: Forgetting await — you get a Promise object, not the value.
Unhandled rejection — always catch, or attach a global handler.
Awaiting inside a loop serially when you meant to run in parallel.

slides.narration: Zooming out. JavaScript itself is single threaded. But the runtime gives you async APIs, and the event loop coordinates the handoff.
A quick history lesson. Every async abstraction built on the one before.
The numbers that drove adoption. Moving from callbacks to async typically cuts code in half with zero runtime cost.
A simple mental picture. Think of the event loop as a conveyor belt — tasks finish, ready jobs get picked up.

data.caption: The event loop constantly checks: anything ready to resume?
data.url: data:image/svg+xml;utf8,<svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 800 320'><rect width='800' height='320' fill='%23161b24'/><rect x='40' y='140' width='720' height='40' rx='20' fill='%230f1218' stroke='%23232a36'/><circle cx='120' cy='160' r='22' fill='%237c9cff'/><circle cx='260' cy='160' r='22' fill='%237c9cff'/><circle cx='400' cy='160' r='22' fill='%239d7cff'/><circle cx='540' cy='160' r='22' fill='%239d7cff'/><circle cx='680' cy='160' r='22' fill='%239d7cff'/><text x='400' y='80' text-anchor='middle' fill='%23e7ecf3' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='24' font-weight='600'>Event loop - tasks move along</text><text x='400' y='260' text-anchor='middle' fill='%2398a2b3' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='14'>ready then pick up then run then repeat</text></svg>

slides.narration: Quick check. What does the await keyword actually do? Hint: it's not what most tutorials say.

data.question: What does `await` do?
choices: Blocks the main thread until the promise resolves
Creates a new thread to wait on the promise
Pauses only the current async function, yielding to the event loop
Converts a callback into a promise
data.explanation: await suspends the async function and registers its continuation as a microtask. The thread keeps running everything else.

slides.narration: Promise chains and async/await are equivalent in power. The difference is ergonomics.
Wrapping up. Async and await are the friendliest face of promises. Use them everywhere, watch the pitfalls, and remember the event loop is still doing the real work.

data.heading: What to take away
points: async/await is syntax over Promises — not a new feature.
await pauses the function, not the thread.
try/catch replaces .catch; Promise.all runs work in parallel.
Forgetting await is the single most common mistake.
